keyword.cluster: prompt injection defense architecture semantic firewall structural isolation multi-layer prevention 2026
keyword.search_volume_estimate: high (6,500–11,000/mo combined cluster, surging with agent adoption)
keyword.competition: medium — category emerging fast, top-ranking pages are thin
keyword.commercial_intent: very high — security teams actively procuring prompt injection defense tools (Lakera Guard, Azure Prompt Shields, LLM Guard)
keyword.trend_momentum: explosive. Prompt injection attacks surged 340% YoY (OWASP 2026). OWASP ranks it #1 for LLMs (LLM01) and #1 for Agents (ASI01) two years running. 88% of organizations report AI agent security incidents. Lakera's November 2025 research demonstrated temporal dissociation — poisoned data corrupting agent memory that activates days or weeks later. 97% of security leaders expect a material AI agent breach in 2026. Cline npm incident (Feb 2026) proved prompt injection + shell access = CI/CD compromise. Microsoft OpenClaw guidance (Feb 2026) explicitly models identity/execution/persistence boundaries. Lakera Guard achieves 98%+ detection at sub-50ms latency.
secondary: Lakera Guard vs Azure Prompt Shields 2026

selection_rationale.why_this_keyword: Prompt injection defense is the natural Part 2 of the AI Agent Security cluster. Part 1 (May 29) established the full threat landscape: OWASP ASI01–ASI10, real-world attacks, and the 5-layer architecture. But every enterprise reader who finished Part 1 has the same question: 'How do I actually defend against prompt injection?' This is the deep-dive that answers that question — the tactical companion to the strategic overview.
timeliness_factors: Prompt injection attacks surged 340% YoY — fastest-growing attack category globally (OWASP 2026)
OWASP ranked prompt injection #1 for LLMs and #1 for Agents — two years running
Lakera's temporal dissociation research (Nov 2025) proved stored context poisoning activates days/weeks later — defense must extend beyond real-time
Cline npm incident (Feb 2026) demonstrated prompt injection + shell access in CI/CD = compromised publication pipeline
Microsoft OpenClaw security guidance (Feb 2026) formalized the identity/execution/persistence boundary model
Gravitee State of AI Agent Security 2026: 88% of orgs report incidents, only 21% have visibility into agent tool access
Lakera Guard (98%+ detection, sub-50ms), Azure Prompt Shields, and LLM Guard are maturing — but no vendor covers all layers
OWASP Q1 2026 Exploit Round-up confirmed transition from theoretical to real-world prompt injection exploitation
selection_rationale.domain_alignment: Direct — AgentForge deploys autonomous AI agents. Prompt injection is our #1 operational risk. This article demonstrates deep domain expertise.
selection_rationale.differentiation_from_prior_runs: Part 2 of the Security cluster. Part 1 (Run #6) was the 30,000-foot strategic view. Part 2 is the 5,000-foot tactical implementation — specific defense techniques, code architecture patterns, vendor comparison, and a progressive breach model. Differentiated from competitor content by the Lakera Progressive Breach Model framing and real-world incident anchoring.
selection_rationale.reader_funnel: CISOs who read Part 1 → security architects who need implementation detail → developers building agent defenses

sections: Executive Summary — the 340% surge stat, why prompt injection is structural not incidental

The Progressive Breach Model (Lakera framing — Phase 1: Compromise Mind → Phase 2: Convert Autonomy → Phase 3: Propagate → Phase 4: Lose Containment)
Seven-Layer Defense Architecture — input sanitization → privilege separation → structural isolation → semantic firewall → runtime enforcement → memory governance → multi-agent trust
Structural Isolation: The Only Defense That Addresses the Root Cause
Semantic Firewalls: Lakera Guard, Azure Prompt Shields, LLM Guard Compared
Memory Governance: Defending Against Temporal Dissociation
Implementation Patterns: Code Architecture for Defense (table: approach vs coverage)
